speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Eli Sommer. Tape number 4.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Sommer, you were telling us about the quarantine period in Gross Rosen and how cold it was there. Could you continue, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Gross Rosen for about-- I don't exactly remember how many days, but it was at least eight days and probably close to two weeks. And then they transported us to a new camp, and-- which was located in Wuestegiersdorf. And that was in Silesia, in German Silesia. It now belongs to Poland, I believe. And it was a new camp, actually. The camp was located in an old porcelain Fabrik, where they used to [? be ?] they used to make porcelain. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was some Hungarian Jews. At that time, they started to bring people from Hungary in camps. And they were in charge. And I remember, the Lagerfuhrer, the German, was-- Schwartz was his name. And actually they sent us again, first the last [? guy who ?] came in [? towards ?] work. And you used go, and we do some work with the stones and with the-- and put in for the train something-- the wooden things for the train. I don't know how you call it. In any case, I was working there for awhile, for a few weeks. And then, somehow we found out they needed people to work at the-- at the-- at the station, train station.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember how I got there. They needed young people who knew how to work, because with Germans, in the wartime especially, very important was that the train should be roaring. Train shouldn't be staying empty for a minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as soon as the train came in with something, it had to be unloaded. And then you have to wait for another train. So they were looking for people who-- young people who would do the job. And somehow I got in. And they were all young people.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were 12 people. And in the beginning, we used to have-- we used to get-- we used to get coals coming in and sand coming in, and nothing attractive for us, at least, because we were always hungry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But after-- and we were working under the Organisation Todt, which was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know how it worked, actually, with the Germans. But they were-- they were getting paid for every-- every person who was working. And then in some cases, there was-- they were sending to work to factories and some places in constructions. We were sending in the Organisation Todt. We were to their disposition. And that was a working organization, Nationale Socialistic, working organization. They had the brown uniforms, and-- with the swastika on the side on the arm. And the guy in charge was a very-- a German, but wasn't Nazi, wasn't a Nazi. But was a German. Work had to be done. And whoever was working good, with him, he was all right. So he was satisfied with us, because when the train came in, we unloaded. And then we had a meister who was overseeing over us. And he was an Austrian. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were always satisfied, because as soon as a train came in, we unload in half an hour, an hour. In the beginning we had one, then two cars load, three car loads. Someday came seven car loads. And we unloaded everything, because when we finished, we were sitting. We were waiting. They couldn't let us go, because they didn't know when the other train would come.

speaker: interviewer
text: So sometimes we can-- we could sit the whole day doing nothing. And sometimes we had to work hard hard-- 7 something became 14. 14 trains came in. And they had to bring additional people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And additional people came from other camps. They didn't have any interest to do this, because they knew when we unload the car, we go home-- we stay doing nothing. But they came in, and they were standing. They never wanted to finish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So sometimes they were sending seven, eight people on to one train, and they never finish one car in the whole day. But for us was very good place, because we liked-- we liked to be. So every now and then, they wanted to send-- to send people to other camps-- to other camps. So they were looking for people to send out. When we heard-- because that was a normal thing. They were sending people from one camp to another.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when we heard that they're talking about sending people to other camp, we came to this German guy from Organisation Todt, and he said-- we told him that they're talking that they want to send us out. And when he heard that they want to send us out, he went, and he called the German in the camp. He said, don't touch our guys. They are the best people I have. And they are doing this and this and that. This way we knew we that we are going to stay. It had to be a politic doing it. And then the Germans were coming back from the East. They were-- the army was coming back, and they were sending back the things from the East. And everything came in from eastern front to Germany. And the-- the train was-- was escorted, always escorted, by SS people. And they had cognac, most of the time-- all the good things, cognac, and cigarettes, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] chocolate. And when they, in our camp, they found out that they have all those things, we had to-- we had to sort it first, not that we had to-- they had to do too much forcing in this respect, because we were trying to help ourselves. And we took the cognac or cigarettes or whatever. And we came in. We brought it into the camp. And for this, we got soup, more soup, more bread. And that's how we could survive pretty easily. Now, don't forget that we had to-- to steal. We had two-- we had the SS guard for us. We had to steal-- the guy from Organisation-- not the German, because he wouldn't think-- he thought that was the biggest crime. But the Austrian, oh, he needed a lot of cigarettes. He was a chain smoker. Then we had to bring to the camp for the German, for the real German, for the-- for the people who are running, the SS from the camp, and the people who are running the camp itself, which are not German. So we had to sneak quite a bit in order to. And they knew exactly when the came, what every train had. And that's how we-- and so the-- my-- our SS man, our guard, had to watch us from the SS man who was coming with a friend, with the train, because when he would find us stealing, that would be the end of whoever stole. So everybody was watching somebody. And that's-- we where there for nine months. So actually, that was, considering that they needed us, considering that we were doing our job pretty good-- I mean, it passed for them, and considering that the things were very valuable that we exchanged for food, everything considering, we were pretty good at that time. Then the evacuation started, Because the German army was coming closer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go onto that, what other nationals were with you in Gross Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gross Rosen, I-- I was-- I was only with-- in Gross Rosen were many nationalities. But in Gross Rosen itself, we were in quarantine only. So we were not-- we were not-- that was separated. It's quarantine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the-- in the camp, we were with the Jewish, Hungarian people, and Polish people-- Jews, Polish, and Hungarian people. And this camp, in this labor camp that you're taken to, this one also?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the one. In Gross Rosen itself, we were on quarantine, so we didn't mix with the-- with the people who were in camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after the nine months of working on the train-- unloading trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were the worst nine months after the ghetto. I consider the ghetto the worst. They put us on a train. And there were about 150 people to one car. And you couldn't stand. You couldn't-- couldn't sit. Everybody was sitting on laps, like-- because they want to squeeze everybody, every inch. And you know, there were some people who were strong. And they felt that they needed more people. And they started to push people around. And we had this group of people, that as I mentioned, about 12 people that were always working, who saw what's going on. And we decided to stay together. And whoever wanted to push us around, to give them a few things. We were still young. And-- and we were the only train that will came to Flossenberg that there was no one killed. Because after we came to Flossenberg, from every train, every car, they took dead people which were trampled, because there were no space, and someone would start pushing around, and it was-- so. But I saw people. I spoke to one person the night before, for instance, and they were on their way. And when we came out, he was completely white. I never saw anything like it. Before, he was normal. And he was completely white. And he was-- you could see that he lost his mind. But nobody was-- nobody-- there was not one body from our car, because we decided to keep sort of-- it was self-preservation. But nobody was-- they started to push around. But they saw they can't do it and that they have-- we wouldn't let them push us around. And nobody was killed. But after we came to Flossenberg, there were a lot of people who were taken out, killed people from the cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: The 12 men that you worked with, where were they all from?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Polish people-- Polish boys, young people. I was one the oldest one. And I was, at the time, 25-- 25.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you arrived to Flossenberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, in quarantine. First, they took us-- whoever arrived in camp, they took to quarantine. They put us separately. And you had to go through certain time and be on guards. And it was bad time. Also in the mountains-- most of the camps that I was, they were always in the mountains. It was very cold. And that was that was in February.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a coat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No coat. We didn't have coats. Coats? Nobody had coats. At that time, you had the pajamas, actually. That's what they wear. And in Flossenberg, we got the new-- new things, new under quarantine. We didn't get coat. We had normal, I guess, used-- could be a Jewish-- Jewish clothing, old clothing. But we got in order-- they had fluorescent paint, like in front and long and then twice here. And the same in the back. When you would try to escape, you could be seen from far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's-- that's-- but no coat. I mean, a jacket and pants. And that's what I went through the end of the war and quarantine. What kind of camp was Flossenberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was concentration camp. That was a mother-- mother camp, like Gross Rosen. It was a real, big camp, where they had-- they-- they-- somebody, for instance, when you were working in quarantine, a lot of people got dysentery. When you've got dysentery, they give you a sign here. And you wore it. That means that you are gone. Next day, you were-- I don't know what they did, if it came so natural when they didn't get food or something. But next day, you were a goner. They found you. The put the dead body in the latrine. And then they-- they had the-- they burned the stoves-- the big stove. It was a real concentration camp. And they burned the bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a crematorium there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Crematorium, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a gas-- was there gassing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were crematorium. I don't know if there were gas. But maybe there was gas. I can't tell you. I was not long enough to be. But I saw a lot of people who got dysentery. And next day, they were dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get sick there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing-- the only time that I really was scared, it was not in Flossenberg. That was when I was in Wuestegiersdorf, when I was working. I had some kind on my head, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some kind of things, like a-- I don't know how to explain-- boils. And I couldn't find-- I didn't know what it was. And I went to a doctor, a Jewish doctor. And I said, tell me what it is, because I was afraid to take off my cap, because the appellplatz twice a day, and when they would notice this, they would send me out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, listen, I can tell you one thing. This is nothing. But you need a some kind of salve. You have to get the salve. And once you get the salve, you'll be all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somehow, I-- as I said, I always had something, because we were unloading things-- and I got the salve for this. And in a few days, went away. That's was only time that I was really scared. Otherwise, I was never sick in camp. I was young. I was always healthy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given a number at any of the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Every camp had a number. I think-- I don't know which camp, whether it was Gross Rosen or Flossenberg that I got the number, or Plaszow. Every camp had a number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the same number of each camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember. But we had the number right here in the front. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I don't remember. I know that after I was liberated, I had the last number. So I remember this. But I don't remember if there were-- if each camp they would change. I would say that they would change. But that's only I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in Flossenberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just quarantine, quarantine-- we didn't do any work. In the morning, that was like the same as Gross Rosen. Got up. It was very cold. It was dark. They pulled us out in front of those things. They wouldn't let us stay in the barracks. And everybody was close to each other because to be warm, because it was very cold. It was snowing. And it was in the mountains. And it was February. And It was rough over there. But it was not too long that we were, I think, two weeks. We were sleeping people four in a-- in a-- in a-- on-- the bed, small bed, very narrow bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. They had soup and two pieces of bread, as far as I remember. That's all we got in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Usually we'd talk about how it used to be that you had a nice breakfast, a nice dinner, and how you used to be home, and what's going to be-- in case we survive, what we are going to do. And how it's going to be heaven when-- when the war will be over and we'll be still alive. But nobody believed in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of the gas chambers at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not in Auschwitz. I knew there gas-- there were chambers. There were, I think. I didn't know at that time they were gas chambers. But we knew that they were killing people. How they were killing, I don't know. I don't know if we remembered that they were the details that we found out later. But people were talking that people were sent out, and nobody survived. And how it happened, nobody knew actually. And we knew when we came to Gross Rosen that they had-- that they had-- there was always fire. You know, the steam was coming out from the-- from the ovens, I guess. And everybody was showing. But I really don't know if we knew, actually, what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you dream about in Flossenberg? What was your dream?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dream, as I said before, the dream-- how it would be if we survived. I knew that my family didn't survive, because at that time, they were talking that anybody who went east didn't survive. They were saying that some policemen, some guards went with the train, and some person from the train, who-- where the trains were going. There were rumors. And rumors, nobody knew-- nobody ever came over and said, listen, I was in this train, and I was in this transport. I knew that people didn't-- they disappeared. So people were guessing. They were saying that nobody's-- those-- whoever were actually the survivors were in camps, in working camps. And then we knew what was going in Auschwitz. Yes, you were hearing about Auschwitz and that people-- what was going on. That they have stoves, and they-- and actually, I didn't know details, no. No, all the details I found out after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anybody very good to you in any of the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't say that anybody was good to me, no. But on the other hand, everybody was looking for survival, himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any of the guards treat you humanely?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I remember, we were-- the only humanely guards were when I was in Wuestegiersdorf. But that time, they were-- all the people already at that time, end of the war, and even '44, the Germans were coming back already. There were pushed out by the Russians. And they know, and they put the young people, old people. So they were a little bit more humane. But I don't know if-- they were talking sometimes like to human beings. But I wouldn't say that they did anything good for me. And I don't know if they could do any good for me. I saw people only through war that they were really bad, because they were just shooting without any reason. They didn't have to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from Flossenberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Flossenberg we went to Ohrdruf.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: They never told us where we were going. That was-- they just put us on the train. And they took us. That's all there was to it. Nobody knew where you are going. And it was always a big secret. And you never know if are you going for life or for death. You arrived, and you went to work, you know that you were alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Ohrdruf?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was end of war, don't forget. That was-- already it must have been March, beginning of March.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945. And we came to Ohrdruf. And they were building a tunnel. We didn't even know what for. We thought it was for the Fabrik, but-- for the-- for the airplanes or for the warehouses. We didn't know at the time what it was. And they were putting us on the lorry and-- for the-- and they were breaking the stones and bringing the sand and the back and forth. And that was a very hard work. It was not a good camp-- very bad camp. First of all, there was no way of getting additional way to get some kind of additional food. So it was-- I wouldn't consider this a good camp. I was there only two or three weeks, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there men and women in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only men. I was never with women-- only men. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think in Prussia were women. Otherwise I don't remember being with women.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what nationals were in Ohrdruf?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ohrdruf, were all-- a lot of Russians, army men, I believe. They was-- they were-- and that was the first camp that I was a really put together. They had, like, 10 people-- it's a wide-- 10 by 10, I believe. Like a [INAUDIBLE], like a-- and 10 people were sleeping in one-- one big space. And-- and there were a lot of Russians there, yes, lot of Russians. And every morning they took away-- they gave us-- they took away the blankets. And in the evening, they gave us another blanket. Nobody had their own blanket. And there were a lot of-- lot of flies and lot of-- first time that I really got infested.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a few weeks. They were-- for liquidation. Whenever we go-- we went at that time, they were liquidating, because they were pushing. They didn't-- they had all the-- the army was coming closer and closer. The Russians were coming closer and closer. And they were going east-- west, all the time west. And they were liquidating the camps. And when you came into camps, they didn't have where to put you. From Ohrdruf they sent us walking to Buchenwald, which was not too far. I remember, I went through Weimar. And they were bombarding the city, the Allied planes. And on the way, there were, like bombs were coming. And then Germans were shooting a prisoner, you know, one of us. And a woman who saw what was going, she started from the window-- because we're going through a town-- and she was yelling and crying when she saw that they were shooting the prisoners. It was really something.

speaker: interviewer
text: What we she yelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were shooting a prisoner in Weimar when we were passing. I don't know for what. There was nothing for us, I mean. But she saw it. And she was yelling, crying, you know? And the bombs were falling, like-- like it there be altogether, as I remember. And we came to Buchenwald. Buchenwald was about 12 kilometers from Weimar.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue in the next tape.
